elements.sent-2411-001: Silicon Photonics-based AI Accelerators (SPAAs) have been considered as promising AI accelerators achieving high energy efficiency and low latency.
elements.sent-2411-002: While many researchers focus on improving SPAAs’ energy efficiency and latency, their physical security has not been sufficiently studied.
elements.sent-2411-003: This paper first proposes a threat of thermal fault injection attacks on SPAAs based on Vector-Matrix Multipliers (VMMs) utilizing Mach-Zhender Interferometers.
elements.sent-2411-004: This paper then proposes SecONN, an optical neural network framework that is capable of not only inferences but also concurrent detection of the attacks.
elements.sent-2411-005: In addition, this paper introduces a concept of Wavelength Division Perturbation (WDP) where wavelength dependent VMM results are utilized to increase detection accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-006: Simulation results show that the proposed method achieves 88.7\% attack-caused average misprediction recall.
elements.sent-2411-007: Artificial Intelligence (AI)-based applications play an important role in the rapid development of our super smart society.
elements.sent-2411-008: Downscaling of CMOS transistors have continuously met ever-increasing demands for computing efficiency in our society.
elements.sent-2411-009: However, the improvement of CMOS circuit latency has been saturated since the parasitic delay becomes dominant in advanced process technologies \cite{irds2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-010: Optical circuits have been emerged as a promising approach for resolving the latency issue of CMOS circuits.
elements.sent-2411-011: Traditionally, optical communication technologies have been utilized for long-distance efficient communication.
elements.sent-2411-012: Recent advancement in silicon photonics has enabled ever-shorter optical network-on-chip applications \cite{onoc .}
elements.sent-2411-013: Recently, optical computing by silicon photonics has been extensively studied to boost up the computational efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-014: Motivated by the rapid AI development, various types of Optical Neural Networks (ONNs) have been widely studied.
elements.sent-2411-015: This paper proposes SecONN, an optical neural network framework that can perform not only inference operations but also concurrent detection of thermal fault injection attacks.
elements.sent-2411-016: Recently, many start-up companies such as Lightmatter \cite{lightmatter , Luminous and LightOn have released commercial photonics-based AI accelerators, which implies that consumers can employ Silicon Photonics-based AI Accelerators (SPAAs) everywhere in the near future.}
elements.sent-2411-017: This enables optics-based edge computing where optical computing devices are employed into midrange edge servers along with mobile devices in order to meet the ever-increasing demand for AI performance as pointed in \cite{kitayama2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-018: The problem in such environments is attackers can also access SPAAs for malicious purposes.
elements.sent-2411-019: For example, Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) are widely used in mission-critical and security-sensitive systems such as autonomous driving systems.
elements.sent-2411-020: Attackers can disrupt the systems by simply injecting a tiny fault that causes the DNN to misclassify the input data as random wrong categories \cite{dnn_fi , which leads to serious accidents in the mission-critical systems.}
elements.sent-2411-021: Another problem is that optical circuit elements are several orders of magnitude larger than CMOS ones.
elements.sent-2411-022: This motivates attackers to access SPAAs for malicious purposes.
elements.sent-2411-023: However, physical security of SPAAs has not been studied sufficiently yet.
elements.sent-2411-024: This paper thus firstly points out a threat of thermal fault injection attacks on SPAAs which causes misprediction of SPAAs.
elements.sent-2411-025: To tackle this issue, a concurrent attack detection technique is proposed.
elements.sent-2411-026: The contribution of this paper is summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-027: This paper introduces a threat of thermal fault injection attacks on SPAAs.
elements.sent-2411-028: The measurement result shows only a single point thermal attack can fully tamper with the cross/bar state of a Mach-Zhender Interferometer (MZI), a core optical element of SPAAs.
elements.sent-2411-029: This indicates SPAAs may mispredict due to the attack.
elements.sent-2411-030: To the best of our knowledge, this is the first paper showing measurement results of thermal fault injection attacks.
elements.sent-2411-031: This paper proposes SecONN featuring a concurrent monitoring technique of thermal fault injection attacks without accuracy degradation.
elements.sent-2411-032: A checksum added in the output layer monitors abnormal phase shifts caused by thermal fault injection attacks without any hardware overhead in optical circuits.
elements.sent-2411-033: In addition, this paper introduces a concept of Wavelength Division Perturbation (WDP) where wavelength-dependent Vector Matrix Multiplication (VMM) results are utilized for detection.
elements.sent-2411-034: This technique amplifies the effect of abnormal phase shifts in VMMs, thereby increasing detection accuracy without degrading ONNs' latency.
elements.sent-2411-035: A simulation environment of thermal fault injection is developed.
elements.sent-2411-036: Simulation results show SecONN achieves 88.7\% attack-caused average misprediction recall.
elements.sent-2411-037: The rest of this paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-038: Section~\ref{sec:motivation presents motivational examples and related works.}
elements.sent-2411-040: In Sect.~\ref{sec:results , evaluation results of SecONN are presented.}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[scale=0.9]{spaa_tiny-crop.pdf} \caption{(a) Overview of SPAA architecture using MZI-VMM. (b) MZI-VMM structure. (c) MZI structure.} \label{fig:mzi}
elements.sent-2411-042: Figure~\ref{fig:mzi a shows an overview of MZI-based SPAA architectures \cite{Shen2017 .}}
elements.sent-2411-043: It consists of an optical VMM part and a digital activation part which correspond to linear transformations and non-linear transformations, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-044: The digital activation part is processed by a CMOS coprocessor.
elements.sent-2411-045: Fully Connected (FC) layers of neural networks can be represented as VMMs where the weight values of FC layers are set as matrix elements.
elements.sent-2411-046: The matrix is denoted as $W$ in this paper.
elements.sent-2411-047: The optical VMM part obtains the VMM result at the $L$'s layer: \begin{eqnarray}y^L = W x^L, \label{eq:vmm}\end{eqnarray} where $x^L$ and $y^L$ are complex amplitudes of input optical signals and output optical signals, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-048: The VMM part can be decomposed as $U$, $\Sigma$, $V^{H}$ and phase shift by the Singular Value Decomposition (SVD).
elements.sent-2411-050: For the VMM part, this paper utilizes an MZI-array Vector-Matrix Multiplier (MZI-VMM) as a representative of SPAAs.
elements.sent-2411-051: Figure~\ref{fig:mzi b shows an MZI-VMM structure.}
elements.sent-2411-052: The two unitary matrices ($U$ and $V^{H}$) are constructed by cascading MZI arrays.
elements.sent-2411-053: The Clements mesh~\cite{Clements2016 is employed for constructing $U$ and $V^{H}$.}
elements.sent-2411-054: The diagonal matrix ($\Sigma$) consists of attenuators or amplifiers.
elements.sent-2411-055: The optical circuits corresponding to $U$ and $V^{H}$ are composed of multiple MZIs (white squares in Fig.~\ref{fig:mzi b).}
elements.sent-2411-056: MZI consists of two phase shifters and two 3~dB couplers as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:mzi c.}
elements.sent-2411-057: An MZI realizes arbitrary $2 \times 2$ unitary matrix multiplication by tuning $\phi$ and $\theta$.
elements.sent-2411-058: By individually tuning $\phi$s and $\theta$s of MZIs in an MZI-VMM, arbitrary VMM can be achieved.
elements.subsection-2411-002: Motivational Example: Thermal Fault Injection Attack
elements.sent-2411-059: This section shows the actual measurement result of thermal fault injection attacks and their impact on ONNs as a motivational example.
elements.sent-2411-060: Figure~\ref{fig:attack1 a shows a photograph of a silicon photonics chip employing a digital switch.}
elements.sent-2411-061: Its equivalent circuit is shown at the bottom of Fig.~\ref{fig:attack1 a.}
elements.sent-2411-062: Two coherent light signals with the same power ($P_0$) are fed into two input ports, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-063: A phase shifter consisting of a PIN injection diode is on a waveguide.
elements.sent-2411-064: The phase-modulated lights are coupled at the 3~dB coupler and then fed into output ports ``O1" and ``O2." By tuning ``Phase shifter," the ``O1" and the ``O2" signals can be fully switched.
elements.sent-2411-065: Figure~\ref{fig:attack1 b shows the measurement results where the horizontal axis and the vertical axis are the bias voltage of the PIN diode and the output light power, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-066: The black broken lines are the tuning result of ``Phase shifter." This shows that the output ports ``O1" and ``O2" are fully switched.
elements.sent-2411-067: In typical optical circuits, near the 3~dB coupler, a thermal heater is mounted for finely tuning the phase offset based on the thermo-optic effect.
elements.sent-2411-068: We assume that the attacker can access the heater to maliciously modulate the phase.
elements.sent-2411-069: The red solid lines of Fig.~\ref{fig:attack1 b shows fault injection results where the bar/cross state of the switch is fully inverted.}
elements.sent-2411-070: As shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:mzi , SPAA consists of MZIs with phase shifters and couplers.}
elements.sent-2411-071: This indicates that attackers can tamper with the entire matrix elements by accessing heaters in SPAAs.
elements.sent-2411-072: Even without directly accessing heaters, attackers can locally heat any waveguides by Thermal Laser Stimulation (TLS) \cite{tls .}
elements.sent-2411-073: Optical beam can locally heat semiconductor devices, thereby resulting in the same tampering result.
elements.sent-2411-074: This means attackers can inject phase shift into any waveguide points.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[scale=0.31]{attack1-crop.pdf} \caption{Measurement result of a thermal fault injection attack.} \label{fig:attack1}
elements.sent-2411-075: To evaluate the tamper resistance of ONNs, this paper builds up the ONN simulator considering the local thermal fault injection attack as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:attack_on_spaa .}
elements.sent-2411-076: Although the detailed structure of the simulator and the ONN is written in Sect.~\ref{sec:framework , this section briefly explains the impact of the thermal fault injection attack.}
elements.sent-2411-077: We assume that the attacker can inject a $\pi$-radian phase offset to an arbitrary single phase shifter of an SPAA with a well-trained model (97.8\% accuracy without attacks) targeting the MNIST handwritten digit dataset \cite{mnist .}
elements.sent-2411-078: Without attacks, the SPAA can correctly classify an input image with a label ``5." If the attacker can somehow find a weak point, a single thermal fault injection attack can tamper with the intensity of the output nodes as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:attack_on_spaa .}
elements.sent-2411-079: The output label with the maximum intensity becomes ``7." The ONN thus mispredicts the input image as ``7." If attackers randomly inject single faults to the SPAA, the average accuracy drops to 93.8\%.
elements.sent-2411-080: In the worst case, the accuracy drops to 2.5\%.
elements.sent-2411-081: Reference~\cite{mwcas considers a similar but different situation where temperature is uniformly increased throughout the chip.}
elements.sent-2411-082: This can be monitored by just placing a temperature sensor near an SPAA macro.
elements.sent-2411-083: However, the thermal fault injection attacks only locally heat specific points in SPAA macors, which makes it hard to detect the attack by a temperature sensor.
elements.sent-2411-084: To tackle this stealthiness issue, this paper proposes an ONN framework with concurrent detection of thermal fault injection attacks.
elements.sent-2411-085: Fault Attacks (FAs) are serious physical attacks where intentional faults are induced in cryptographic circuits and secret key information is revealed by analyzing the erroneous output and the correct output.
elements.sent-2411-086: The concept of FAs was firstly proposed by \cite{fa_boneh .}
elements.sent-2411-087: In order to induce intentional faults, for example, attackers use abnormal clock signals \cite{fukunaga_clock_2009 and voltage fluctuation \cite{guilley_voltage_2008 .}}
elements.sent-2411-088: A laser fault injection attack has also emerged as a powerful FA method \cite{lfi where faults can be accurately injected by laser beams to flip-flops at arbitral specific positions in chips.}
elements.sent-2411-089: Although FAs were originally proposed to attack cryptographic circuits, it is now recognized that attackers can use FAs for inducing misprediction of DNN-based applications.
elements.sent-2411-090: DNNs are widely used in mission-critical and security-sensitive systems such as autonomous driving systems.
elements.sent-2411-091: If attackers can induce misprediction of DNNs in such systems, these attacks can cause serious damage to the systems.
elements.sent-2411-092: To induce intentional faults, attackers tamper with parameters stored in memories \cite{dnn_fi or with activation results \cite{dnn_laser_fi .}}
elements.sent-2411-093: Basic countermeasures are to test the circuits \cite{fi_testing , to employ fault-tolerant redundant data formats \cite{fi_redundant and to employ sensors of FAs \cite{8474958 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-094: The problem is ONNs are an analog optical computation paradigm.
elements.sent-2411-095: Conventional testing and fault-tolerant approaches are for digital CMOS circuits.
elements.sent-2411-096: It thus can not be directly utilized for analog SPAAs.
elements.sent-2411-097: Redundant analog formats significantly increase the hardware cost.
elements.sent-2411-098: Another problem is it is hard to directly use conventional CMOS-based FA sensor techniques for non-CMOS optics-based elements.
elements.sent-2411-099: This paper proposes a runtime detection technique of thermal fault injection attacks on ONNs.
elements.sent-2411-100: The physical security of optical circuits has not been fully studied so far.
elements.sent-2411-101: References~\cite{Naruse:07,shiomi_2021 point out optical circuits have the potential to achieve tamper-resistant logical operations against side-channel attacks which reveal secret information by measuring optical signals inside optical circuits.}
elements.sent-2411-102: Although their techniques work well for side-channel attacks, they do not target how to protect against invasive FAs targeted in this paper.
elements.sent-2411-103: Reference~\cite{adversarial points out that adversarial attacks on SPAAs, where small noises are injected into input images to induce misprediction, are theoretically possible.}
elements.sent-2411-104: However, they do not point out how to protect SPAAs against adversarial attacks.
elements.sent-2411-105: Reference~\cite{mwcas points out the SPAAs' vulnerability to thermal cross talk.}
elements.sent-2411-106: The malicious optical circuits can increase their own temperature.
elements.sent-2411-107: Thus, the temperature of the victim optical circuits near the malicious one is increased by the thermal cross talk, which induces erroneous outputs.
elements.sent-2411-108: Reference~\cite{mwcas proposes a simple attack detection method.}
elements.sent-2411-109: Test signals are fed into the ONNs, and the output signals are compared with the expected outputs.
elements.sent-2411-110: If we simply utilize this technique to detect thermal fault injection attacks, this paper points out that it will degrade SPAAs' potential performance.
elements.sent-2411-111: Without the degradation, this paper targets the detection method of thermal fault injection attacks which are more aggressive invasive attacks on SPAAs.
elements.sent-2411-112: Assuming that attackers can access SPAAs, this paper targets the threat model where attackers can locally inject single phase shift errors into arbitral phase shifters in SPAAs.
elements.sent-2411-113: Specifically, the attacker injects a phase error into one of phase shifters in an SPAA ($\theta$s and $\phi$s in Fig.~\ref{fig:mzi c) during the inference operation of each input data.}
elements.sent-2411-114: Considering the situation for mission-critical systems, attackers attempt to cause DNNs to misclassify the input data as random wrong categories for inducing serious damage to the systems.
elements.sent-2411-115: This paper proposes SecONN, a neural network framework that is capable of concurrently detecting thermal fault injection attacks.
elements.sent-2411-116: Figure~\ref{fig:overview shows the overview of the framework.}
elements.sent-2411-117: This framework features the following three flows: (1) Training, (2) Weight conversion and (3) Inference.
elements.sent-2411-118: This technique is presented in Sect.~\ref{sec:seconn .}
elements.sent-2411-119: In ``(2) Weight conversion," weight values in ONNs are converted to phase shift values of MZIs ($\phi$s and $\theta$s in Fig.~\ref{fig:mzi c).}
elements.sent-2411-120: This paper proposes a concept of Weavelength Division Perturbation (WDP) where abnormal phase shifts are amplified by feeding multiple lights with different wavelengths.
elements.sent-2411-121: SecONN also features a simulation environment which is firstly presented in Sect.~\ref{sec:simulation .}
elements.sent-2411-122: The proposed framework converts weight values of target neural network into phase shift values of MZIs ($\phi$s and $\theta$s in Fig.~\ref{fig:mzi c).}
elements.sent-2411-123: The simulator then obtains optical characteristics (amplitudes and phases) of output lights in each layer considering thermal fault injection attacks.
elements.sent-2411-124: This paper extends AnalogVNN~\cite{analogvnn , a simulation framework for optical neural networks.}
elements.sent-2411-126: AnalogVNN comprises a Digital-Analog Conversion (DAC) layer, a linear Operation (``Conv/FC") layer, and an Analog-Digital Converson (ADC) layer.
elements.sent-2411-127: DAC and ADC layers consist of normalization, precision reduction, and noise addition.
elements.sent-2411-128: The latter two layers quantize the inputs and add the Gaussian noise to them, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-129: In ``Conv/FC," VMM for a target layer ($L$'th layer in this case) is performed based on preprocessed analog inputs and weight values.
elements.sent-2411-130: AnalogVNN itself only numerically calculates VMM expressed in Eq.~(\ref{eq:vmm ) during the linear operation.}
elements.sent-2411-131: AnalogVNN does not consider thermal fault injections.
elements.sent-2411-132: In order to evaluate thermal fault injections, firstly $W$ in Eq.~(\ref{eq:vmm ) needs to be decomposed to a set of phase shift values in each MZI ($\phi$s and $\theta$s in Fig.~\ref{fig:mzi c).}}
elements.sent-2411-133: Generally, a small MZI-VMM needs to be reutilized multiple times for obtaining the entire VMM result due to the hardware resource limitation.
elements.sent-2411-134: This is not also considered in AnalogVNN but needs to be considered since fault injection points in the MZI-VMM need to be set for evaluating the tamper resistance.
elements.figure-2411-006: \begin{minipage}{\linewidth} \includegraphics[scale=0.9]{base_arch_tiny-crop.pdf} \subcaption{The target SPAA architecture.} \label{fig:basearch} \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}{\linewidth} \includegraphics[scale=0.9]{modeling_tiny-crop.pdf} \subcaption{The layer design of Fig. \ref{fig:basearch}.} \label{fig:modeling} \end{minipage} \caption{The target SPAA and its layer design.}
elements.sent-2411-135: To evaluate the impact of thermal fault injection attacks on devices, the decomposition flow is implemented to AnalogVNN.
elements.sent-2411-136: The flow is as follows: (1) Model training without MZI-VMM simulation (numerical optimization of $W$ in Eq.~(\ref{eq:vmm ) only), (2) Conversion of the weights for the MZI-VMM (decomposition to $\phi$s and $\theta$s in Fig.~\ref{fig:mzi c), and (3) Inference operation with the MZI-VMM simulator.}}
elements.sent-2411-137: In (2) and (3), this paper targets the optical VMM architecture illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:basearch .}
elements.sent-2411-138: This architecture has a set of MZI-VMM of matrix size $N$ and homodyne detectors.
elements.sent-2411-139: It numerically calculates MZI-VMM results $RC/N^2$ times, where $R$ and $C$ are the numbers of rows and columns of the matrix $W$ in Eq.~(\ref{eq:vmm ), respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-140: The multiplication results are accumulated digitally to obtain the $R\times C$-size VMM result.
elements.sent-2411-141: The input laser and the output signals of the ADC are quantized with $A$-bit precision.
elements.sent-2411-142: Similarly, the signals to set weights are quantized.
elements.sent-2411-144: We replace the linear layer in Fig.~\ref{fig:analoglayer with the in-house MZI-VMM simulator \cite{kawakamisim .}}
elements.sent-2411-145: This simulator decomposes weight values to $\phi$s and $\theta$s in Fig.~\ref{fig:mzi c by the decomposition flow of \cite{Clements2016 .}}
elements.sent-2411-146: This paper utilizes balanced output partitions presented in \cite{balanced_output for concurrently detecting thermal fault injection attacks.}
elements.sent-2411-147: It is originally designed to concurrently detect hardware faults such as single event upsets in neural networks operating for safety-critical digital systems.
elements.sent-2411-148: This paper proposes that this technique can be utilized for detecting thermal fault injection attacks on analog SPAAs.
elements.sent-2411-149: Balanced output partitions are designed to embed a balance checksum in deep neural networks.
elements.sent-2411-150: This paper adds an additional node with fixed weights in each layer as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:checksum .}
elements.sent-2411-151: The first half edges have a $+1$ weight value while the second half edges have a $-1$ weight value.
elements.sent-2411-152: If the checksum result exceeds a predetermined threshold, it is judged that the ONN is attacked.
elements.sent-2411-153: We assume the checksum for this additional node can be performed by a CMOS coprocessor.
elements.sent-2411-154: No additional hardware is required for optical circuits.
elements.sent-2411-155: In this paper, we implement this method into the output layer as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:training .}
elements.sent-2411-157: We omit the ``Reduce Precision" and the ``Noise" layers in the original ``DAC" and ``ADC" layers to boost up the training speed in Fig.~\ref{fig:analoglayer .}
elements.sent-2411-158: Weights and biases are quantized with 4~bits.
elements.sent-2411-159: The detail of the inference phase is described in Sect.~\ref{sec:wdm .}
elements.figure-2411-007: \begin{minipage}{\linewidth} \includegraphics[scale=0.9]{balanceoutput-crop.pdf} \subcaption{Balanced output partitions.} \label{fig:checksum} \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}{\linewidth} \includegraphics[scale=0.61]{training_revise-crop.pdf} \subcaption{Training architecture.} \label{fig:training} \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}{\linewidth} \includegraphics[scale=0.61]{inference_tiny_js-crop.pdf} \subcaption{Inference and runtime detection architecture ($\lambda$: Wavelength).} \label{fig:inference} \end{minipage} \caption{SecONN training/inference architecture.}
elements.sent-2411-160: The balanced output partitions do not detect a fault injection attack if the attack does not impact on checksum results.
elements.sent-2411-161: For example, if the attack increases the sum of the first half and the sum of the second half of the output valuses in Fig.~\ref{fig:checksum by the same amount, the checksum result does not change, which leads to misdetection.}
elements.sent-2411-162: To increase the detection capability, a concept of WDP is proposed.
elements.sent-2411-163: The key point is $\phi$s and $\theta$s in Fig.~\ref{fig:mzi c depend on the light wavelength.}
elements.sent-2411-164: We input light signals with the same intensities but different wavelengths to ONNs.
elements.sent-2411-165: As a result, $W$ in Eq.~(\ref{eq:vmm ) is ``perturbed" and transformed into a matrix with different element values.}
elements.sent-2411-166: This changes the balance at the checksum node, thereby amplifying the effect of the abnormal phase shift even if the attack does not impact on checksum results with the original wavelength.
elements.sent-2411-167: WDP allows SecONN not only to perform inference in a target wavelength but also to detect attacks in all the wavelengths concurrently, which does not impact on the operating speed of the main inference operation.
elements.sent-2411-168: In order to separately get the checksum results, the number of checksum nodes in the output layer (``$\Sigma$" node in Fig.~\ref{fig:checksum ) is set to be equal to the number of wavelengths.}
elements.sent-2411-169: Figure~\ref{fig:inference shows a case where there are 3 wavelengths ($\lambda_1$, $\lambda_2$ and $\lambda_3$) and three checksum nodes (``$\Sigma$"s) are used.}
elements.sent-2411-170: The optical signal with $\lambda_1$ provides not only a VMM result but also a checksum result.
elements.sent-2411-171: The other two input signals with $\lambda_2$, $\lambda_3$ have the same intensities as the original $\lambda_1$ signal.
elements.sent-2411-172: They are only concurrently used for obtaining checksum results.
elements.sent-2411-173: Since $W$ elements differ by wavelengths, the three checksum results are different, thereby increasing the detectability.
elements.sent-2411-174: If at least one of the checksum results exceeds a predetermined threshold, it is judged that the ONN is attacked.
elements.sent-2411-175: The threshold is set to a positive number to reduce the misdetection (false negative) rate.
elements.sent-2411-176: This is because the checksum nodes alert for every inference operation if the threshold is too low (e.g., 0).
elements.sent-2411-177: The following features are implemented into the proposed simulator in order to incorporate the WDP effect: Feature1: Phase shifters are the only wavelength-dependent elements.
elements.sent-2411-178: This can be achieved assuming that all the elements are symmetrically placed and that broadband couplers such as \cite{broadbandDC are utilized for MZIs.}
elements.sent-2411-179: Feature2: Process variation impacts are negligible.
elements.sent-2411-180: Feature3: Phase shifters have wavelength dependency.
elements.sent-2411-181: The dependency is defined as $\theta(\lambda)=\eta(\lambda)\theta_{\rm set}$, where $\lambda$, $\theta$, $\theta_{\rm set}$ denote the wavelength, the actual amount of the phase shift and the phase shift we set, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-182: Detailed parameters of $\eta(\lambda)$ are based on actual silicon photonics chip parameters.
elements.sent-2411-183: Roughly speaking, $\eta(\lambda)$ is a function which is proportional to $\lambda^{-1}$.
elements.sent-2411-184: Note that the key point is $W$ in Eq.~(\ref{eq:vmm ) depends on wavelength.}
elements.sent-2411-185: We thus only limit the wavelength-dependent elements to phase shifters in this paper for simplicity.
elements.sent-2411-186: We apply the proposed method to MNIST handwritten digit dataset classification \cite{mnist .}
elements.sent-2411-187: A handwritten number of $28\times28$ pixels is reshaped to an array of $784\times1$ and is then input into the network.
elements.sent-2411-188: The neural network has three FCs with LeakyReLU as an activation function.
elements.sent-2411-190: The FCs transform the feature dimensions from 784 to 256, from 256 to 128, and from 128 to 10, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-191: The training dataset is split into training sets of 55000 examples and validation sets of 5000 examples by the stratified sampling.
elements.sent-2411-193: The recognition accuracy achieves 97.8\% with the simulation.
elements.sent-2411-194: Since the recognition accuracy of the model without balanced output partitions is 97.7\%, the proposed concurrent detection does not degrade the model quality.
elements.sent-2411-195: The threshold is determined so that the misdetection (false negative) rate is 5\% without attacks.

elements.sent-2411-196: The device setup is shown in TABLE~\ref{tab:devicesetup .}
elements.sent-2411-197: This simulation adds the Gaussian noise with 0.02 standard deviation to the signals.
elements.sent-2411-198: This simulation also quantizes 4-bit in laser sources and homodyne detection.
elements.sent-2411-199: MZI-VMM can perform $16\times16$ sized matrix and the weight signals quantized 4 bit.
elements.sent-2411-200: We use $1550\pm 50$~nm in WDP (i.e., $\lambda_1=1550~{\rm nm}$, $\lambda_2=1500~{\rm nm}$, $\lambda_3=1600~{\rm nm}$ in Fig.~\ref{fig:inference ).}
elements.sent-2411-201: We assume one phase shifter in an SPAA is attacked during the inference operation of every sample.
elements.sent-2411-202: Phase shifts of $\pi/2$, $\pi$ and $3\pi/2$ radian are injected to every phase shifter.
elements.sent-2411-203: For each injection, the checksums and the detection accuracy are evaluated with test datasets containing 10000 samples.

elements.sent-2411-252: While many researchers focus on improving SPAAs’ energy efficiency and latency, their physical security has only recently received attention.
elements.sent-2411-253: While it is essential to deliver strong optical neural network inferencing approaches, their success and adoption are predicated on their ability to deliver a secure execution environment.
elements.sent-2411-254: Towards this end, this paper proposes PrometheusFree, an optical neural network framework that is capable of concurrent detection of laser fault injection attacks.
elements.sent-2411-255: This paper first presents an illustrative threat of laser fault injection attacks on SPAAs, capable of subjecting the optical neural network to misclassifications.
elements.sent-2411-256: The threat then is addressed in this paper by developing techniques for concurrent detection of the laser fault injection attacks.
elements.sent-2411-257: Furthermore, this paper introduces a novel application of Wavelength Division Perturbation (WDP) technique where wavelength-dependent Vector Matrix Multiplication (VMM) results are utilized to boost fault attack detection accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-258: Simulation results show that PrometheusFree achieves over 96\% attack-caused misprediction recall as the use of the WDP technique squashes the attack success rate by 38.6\% on average.
elements.sent-2411-259: Compared with prior art, PrometheusFree limits the average attack success ratio to 0.019, yielding a 95.3\% reduction.
elements.sent-2411-260: The experimental results confirm the superiority of the concurrent detection and the boost in attack detection abilities imparted by the WDP approaches.
elements.sent-2411-261: The continuous downscaling of CMOS transistors has been exploited heretofore to satisfy the ever-increasing demands for computing efficiency in our society.
elements.sent-2411-262: However, improvements in CMOS circuit latency have saturated since parasitic delay has become dominant in advanced process technologies \cite{irds2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-263: Optical circuits have emerged as a promising approach for resolving the latency wall of CMOS circuits.
elements.sent-2411-264: Recent advancements in silicon photonics have enabled ever-shorter optical network-on-chip applications \cite{onoc .}
elements.sent-2411-265: Recently, optical computing by silicon photonics has been extensively studied to boost up computational efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-266: Motivated largely by the needs of the rapid emerging AI applications, various types of Optical Neural Networks (ONNs) have been widely studied \cite{Fu2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-267: Recently, many start-up companies such as Lightmatter \cite{lightmatter , Luminous, LightOn, Lightelligence and Q.ANT have released commercial silicon photonics-based AI accelerators (SPAAs), signaling their imminent widespread adoption.}
elements.sent-2411-268: This development foreshadows optics-based edge computing with optical computing devices employed as midrange edge servers along with mobile devices to meet the ever-increasing demand for AI performance as pointed out in \cite{kitayama2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-269: In the research direction of photonic computing, \cite{edgephotonics presents a proof-of-concept demonstration claiming DNNs can be executed on photonic devices on both edge devices and servers.}
elements.sent-2411-270: The question of mitigating the vulnerabilities of ONNs to physical security attacks, however, has received attention only recently, putting the viability of the adoption of ONNs as a supporting technology for AI applications into sharp question.
elements.sent-2411-271: A fundamental challenge that would need to be addressed to solve the issues raised is the exposure to attackers that can access SPAAs for malicious purposes.
elements.sent-2411-272: In these contexts, the frequently presumed physical protection falls short as unprotected edge servers can be easily accessed by an attacker leading to widespread client malfunctions possibly.
elements.sent-2411-273: The injection of even a tiny fault could cause the DNN to misclassify the input data \cite{dnn_fi , leading to serious accidents in mission-critical systems.}
elements.sent-2411-274: A further challenge is the sheer size of optical circuit elements, typically several orders of magnitude larger than CMOS ones, motivating attackers to access SPAAs for malicious purposes due to the facile and precise identification and manipulation of the target elements.
elements.sent-2411-275: This paper promises to unbound the shackles of security vulnerability of ONNs by introducing PrometheusFree, an optical neural network framework that can perform concurrent detection of laser fault injection attacks.
elements.sent-2411-276: Given the urgency and criticality of SPAA attack vulnerability, this paper targets the thermal vulnerability of SPAAs, a viable laser fault injection attack.
elements.sent-2411-277: This paper goes on to introduce a capable defense to protect against the attack vulnerability identified.
elements.sent-2411-278: The concrete contributions of this paper are summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-279: This paper introduces a threat of laser fault injection attacks on SPAAs.
elements.sent-2411-280: The measurement result shows even a single laser beam can inject a $\pi$ phase shift into an SPAA, which is sufficient to cause ONN misprediction.
elements.sent-2411-281: This paper proposes PrometheusFree, featuring a concurrent monitoring technique of laser fault injection attacks, yet incurring no accuracy degradation.
elements.sent-2411-282: A checksum added in the model layers monitors abnormal phase shifts caused by laser fault injection attacks in optical circuits.
elements.sent-2411-283: This paper introduces a concept of Wavelength Division Perturbation (WDP) where wavelength-dependent Vector Matrix Multiplication (VMM) results are utilized for detection.
elements.sent-2411-284: This technique amplifies the effect of abnormal phase shifts in VMMs, thereby increasing detection accuracy without degrading ONNs' latency, while incurring minimal hardware overhead.
elements.sent-2411-285: A simulation environment of fault injection attacks targeted in this paper is developed.
elements.sent-2411-286: Simulation results show PrometheusFree achieves on average over 96\% attack-caused misprediction recall.

elements.sent-2411-292: The weight matrix $W$ is decomposed into unitary matrices $U$ and $V^{H}$, diagonal matrix $\Sigma$, and phase shifts by the Singular Value Decomposition (SVD).
elements.sent-2411-293: The diagonal matrix $\Sigma$ consists of attenuators or amplifiers.
elements.sent-2411-294: The optical circuits corresponding to the Clements mesh are composed of multiple MZIs.

elements.subsection-2411-013: Motivational Example: Laser Fault Injection Attacks
elements.sent-2411-298: Fault Injection Attacks (FIAs) are serious physical attacks for inducing misprediction of DNN-based applications.
elements.sent-2411-299: If attackers can induce misprediction in DNNs in such systems, these attacks can end up having serious repercussions on the systems.
elements.sent-2411-300: For traditional CMOS AI accelerators, attackers tamper with parameters stored in memories \cite{dnn_fi or with activation results \cite{dnn_laser_fi using FIAs.}}
elements.figure-2411-012: \includegraphics[width=8cm]{fig/attack-crop.pdf} \caption{Laser fault injection attack on silicon photonics devices.} \label{fig:attack2}
elements.sent-2411-301: This paper proposes a novel threat of laser fault injection attacks on optical circuits.
elements.sent-2411-302: A laser source mounted over a fabricated silicon photonics chip shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:attack2 a noninvasively injects an additional phase into an optical circuit.}
elements.sent-2411-303: Fig.~\ref{fig:attack2 b shows a circuit diagram of a portion of an MZI (an MZI corresponding to the phase shifter in Fig.~\ref{fig:mzi yet without a $\phi$).}}
elements.sent-2411-304: Phase shifters based on PIN diodes are used to modulate optical phases (``$\theta$" in Fig.~\ref{fig:mzi ).}
elements.sent-2411-305: Generally, optical circuits employ heaters near couplers to compensate nonideal phase fluctuation introduced by process and environmental variations.
elements.sent-2411-306: When attackers irradiate a laser beam with a 1425~nm wavelength onto a metal plate of a heater, its temperature locally increases.
elements.sent-2411-307: Note that the spot diameter of the laser beam is comparable to the heater size.
elements.sent-2411-308: An additional phase shift is thus injected by the thermo-optic effect.
elements.sent-2411-309: This is equivalent to injecting a phase error to a nearby phase shifter.
elements.sent-2411-310: Fig.~\ref{fig:attack2 c shows a measurement result.}
elements.sent-2411-311: Without a laser beam, the input light goes to the $P_1$ port.
elements.sent-2411-312: However, with a laser beam, the input light passes to the opposite port ($P_2$).
elements.sent-2411-313: This is equivalent to injection of a $\pi$ phase shift, the most aggressive phase shift, into a phase shifter.
elements.sent-2411-314: If the attacker irradiates a laser beam into the heater on the opposite arm, a negative phase shift can be injected.
elements.sent-2411-315: This paper numerically confirms in Section~\ref{sec:4 that ONN misprediction can be induced by a single laser fault injection only.}
elements.sent-2411-316: Emblematic of the analog optical computation paradigm, ONNs impose special challenges, precluding the use of numerous defences that can be employed in digital CMOS circuits to detect attacks, such as the comprehensive testing of the circuits \cite{fi_testing , the use of fault-tolerant redundant data formats \cite{fi_redundant and the utilization of sensors for FIAs \cite{8474958 as countermeasures for the proposed laser fault injection attacks.}}}
elements.sent-2411-317: The application of such digital methods is highly challenging for analog SPAAs.
elements.sent-2411-318: The redundant data formats no longer work well since the proposed laser fault injection attacks directly tamper with phase shifts of the optical devices.
elements.sent-2411-319: The use of conventional CMOS-based FIA sensor techniques for non-CMOS optics-based elements poses formidable challenges, precluding their facile use as a countermeasure as well.
elements.sent-2411-320: Another physical-level countermeasure would be the use of a metal shield over CMOS transistors by utilizing metal interconnections to protect chips from laser beams.
elements.sent-2411-321: However, the laser beam can be bypassed from the backside of the CMOS chip~\cite{backside .}
elements.sent-2411-322: Silicon photonic fabrication technologies are based on CMOS fabrication technologies.
elements.sent-2411-323: The vulnerability to laser FIAs thus still remains an open question in need of resolution for silicon photonic chips as well.
elements.sent-2411-324: While software-based attacks such as adversarial attacks~\cite{adversarial on SPAAs have been discussed, the concerns of hardware security have also recently received attention.}
elements.sent-2411-325: In general, two major units in SPAAs are targeted: CMOS controllers and optical processing units.
elements.sent-2411-326: SPAAs typically employ electronic controllers to modulate optical circuits.
elements.sent-2411-327: Their modulation configurations are stored in CMOS memories.
elements.sent-2411-328: For these CMOS controllers, \cite{faoptics introduces the phase gradient attacks identifying the optimal abnormal phase in each phase shifter to induce the erroneous inference results.}
elements.sent-2411-329: The memory values can be tampered with by bit-flip attacks as highlighted in~\cite{unlikely .}
elements.sent-2411-330: In addition, \cite{serlos points out a threat of Hardware Trojans (HTs) which are maliciously inserted to the electronic controllers by untrusted entities.}
elements.sent-2411-331: HTs manipulate the propagation direction of the optical signals on MZIs, resulting in erroneous outputs.
elements.sent-2411-332: Malicious electro-optic circuits interfere with optical elements and lead to erroneous inference results.
elements.sent-2411-333: A similar situation is discussed in~\cite{safelight .}
elements.sent-2411-334: For optical computing units, security vulnerabilities caused by thermal crosstalk are pointed out in \cite{serlos .}
elements.sent-2411-335: Malicious optical circuits can increase their own temperature, thus elevating the temperature of the victim optical circuits near them through thermal crosstalk, resulting in erroneous outputs.
elements.sent-2411-336: To tackle these vulnerabilities on SPAAs, \cite{serlos,unlikely,safelight propose countermeasures featuring detection and error-recovery mechanisms.}
elements.sent-2411-337: However, it is hard to use these countermeasures directly to detect laser fault injection attacks on optical circuits.
elements.sent-2411-338: Ref.~\cite{serlos proposes a simple attack detection method where test signals are fed into the ONNs and the output signals are then compared with the expected outputs.}
elements.sent-2411-339: This testing method performed at specific intervals achieves 100\% anomaly detection in the various scenarios, whereas it results in the degradation of the SPAAs' potential performance since SPAAs end up processing test signals frequently in order to monitor the laser fault injection attacks.
elements.sent-2411-340: Moreover, SPAAs are vulnerable if the attackers can distinguish the normal inference phase and the testing phase.
elements.sent-2411-341: The laser fault injection attacks are no longer detectable if attackers can only tamper with SPAAs operating in the normal inference phase.
elements.sent-2411-342: Ref.~\cite{safelight introduces methods for improving robustness of inference operations with little performance overhead even if HTs tamper with internal signals.}
elements.sent-2411-343: While this method can be applied as a countermeasure for laser fault injection attacks shown in Section~\ref{sec:latency , the method falls short of fully compensating for the accuracy degradation if attackers inject aggressive errors such as laser fault injection attacks.}
elements.sent-2411-344: Ref.~\cite{unlikely proposes a recovery method and a robust number representation method against bit-flip attacks on CMOS controllers.}
elements.sent-2411-345: This method utilizes a checksum validation method to detect the bit-flip attacks and a number representation with low sensitivity to the attacks.
elements.sent-2411-346: Since the targeted laser fault injection attacks directly induce analog perturbations in optical circuits, an alternative approach is required to adopt the method outlined in Ref.~\cite{unlikely as a countermeasure against the attacks.}
elements.sent-2411-347: This paper assumes the attackers' motivation is to cause malfunctions in edge photonic computing systems.
elements.sent-2411-349: Considering mission-critical systems as the specific target, attackers attempt to force DNNs to misclassify the input data into random wrong categories thus inducing serious damage to the systems.
elements.sent-2411-350: Attackers access SPAAs implemented on an edge server and locally inject phase shift errors into arbitral phase shifters in SPAAs.
elements.sent-2411-351: Specifically, the attacker injects phase errors into phase shifters in an SPAA ($\phi$'s and $\theta$'s in Fig.~\ref{fig:mzi ) that remain operational throughout the entire inference process for each input data.}
elements.sent-2411-352: This paper outlines PrometheusFree, a neural network framework that is capable of concurrently detecting laser fault injection attacks.

elements.sent-2411-359: The proposed framework converts the weight values of the target neural network into phase shift values of MZIs ($\phi$'s and $\theta$'s in Fig.~\ref{fig:mzi ).}
elements.sent-2411-360: The simulator then obtains optical characteristics (amplitudes and phases) of output lights in each layer considering laser fault injection attacks.

elements.subsection-2411-016: ONN for Concurrent Detection of Laser Fault Injection Attacks
elements.sent-2411-377: This paper utilizes the balanced output partition technique proposed in \cite{balanced_output for concurrently detecting laser fault injection attacks.}
elements.sent-2411-378: While originally designed to concurrently detect hardware faults such as single event upsets in neural networks employed in safety-critical digital systems, this paper explores the efficacy of this technique for detecting laser fault injection attacks on analog SPAAs.
elements.sent-2411-379: This technique adds an additional node with fixed weights in each layer as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:checksum .}
elements.sent-2411-380: Half of the edges have a positive unit weight value while the other half have a negative unit weight value.
elements.sent-2411-381: The weights are trained to minimize its checksum toward zero.
elements.sent-2411-382: In the inference phase, it is deemed that the ONN is being attacked if the checksum result exceeds a predetermined threshold.
elements.sent-2411-383: The lightweight checksum operation for this additional node is performed by a CMOS coprocessor.
elements.sent-2411-384: Our thorough evaluation shows the latency time for the checksum calculation to be considerably smaller compared to the inference latency time of ONNs under practical device parameters.

elements.sent-2411-385: In this paper, we incorporate this method into an analog layer as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:training , which shows the training architecture.}
elements.sent-2411-386: The training architecture has two branches.
elements.sent-2411-387: While the upper branch is used to train the checksums, the lower counterpart focuses on enhancing the inference capability and the robustness of the model.
elements.sent-2411-388: We omit the ``Reduce precision" and the ``Noise" layers in the original ``DAC" and ``ADC" layers in Fig.~\ref{fig:analoglayer to boost the training speed, except for the noise layer on the input side of the lower branch.}
elements.sent-2411-389: The ``Noise'' layer on the lower branch is retained to give the model robustness, as inspired by Safelight~\cite{safelight .}
elements.sent-2411-390: The balanced output partitions do not detect a laser fault injection attack if the attack has no material impact on the checksum results.
elements.sent-2411-391: For example, if the attack increases the sums of the two halves of the output values in Fig.~\ref{fig:checksum by the same amount, the checksum result remains constant, which leads to misdetection (false negative).}
elements.sent-2411-392: To increase the detection capability, a concept of Wavelength Division Perturbation (WDP) is proposed.
elements.sent-2411-393: The key point is that the actual modulation amounts of $\phi$ and $\theta$ in Fig.~\ref{fig:mzi depend on the light wavelength.}
elements.sent-2411-394: We input light signals with identical intensities but different wavelengths to ONNs.
elements.sent-2411-395: As a result, the weight matrix $W$ is ``perturbed" in a wavelength-dependent manner.
elements.sent-2411-396: The intensities of the output lights with multiple wavelengths are thus different.
elements.sent-2411-397: This changes the balance at the checksum node, thereby amplifying the effect of the abnormal phase shift even if the attack does not impact the checksum results on the original wavelength.
elements.sent-2411-398: WDP allows PrometheusFree not only to perform inference in a target wavelength but also to detect attacks in all the wavelengths concurrently, yet has no impact on the operating speed of the main inference operation.

elements.sent-2411-455: Safelight, which utilizes the Noise-Aware Training (NAT) approach, is an applicable approach as a countermeasure to mitigate laser fault injection attacks.
elements.sent-2411-456: NAT is a training method injecting noise to neural network architectures in a training phase.

elements.sent-2411-458: The effects of their malicious activities are considered as noise.
elements.sent-2411-459: By injecting noise in the training phase, Safelight realizes tamper-resilient ONNs.

elements.sent-2411-471: This paper proposes PrometheusFree, an optical neural network framework enabling not only inference operations but also concurrent detection of laser fault injection attacks on optical circuits.
elements.sent-2411-472: PrometheusFree features the concurrent detection of the laser fault injection attacks as well as providing a simulation environment capable of considering the laser fault injection attacks.
elements.sent-2411-473: Furthermore, PrometheusFree employs WDP which fully exploits the nature of light and boosts the detection rate without degrading the operating speed.
elements.sent-2411-474: The evaluation results show that the proposed method achieves over 96\% attack-caused average misprediction recall and squashes the attack success ratio by over 98\% compared with the baseline.
